1: Wine as mocker, strong drink as brawler, and those led astray by them lacking wisdom. The Nazarite vow and priestly abstinence traditions found their fulfillment in Christ’s self-discipline: though he turned water into wine (John 2:1-11), he drank from the cup of suffering (Matthew 26:42) and refused the wine mingled with gall at the cross (Matthew 27:34). His final abstinence — “I will not drink again of this fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father’s kingdom” (Matthew 26:29) — is the eschatological restraint that shapes the use of all earthly pleasures.
2: The king’s terror like a lion’s roar — provoking his anger is forfeiting your life. Revelation 5:5 and Proverbs 6:16-17 present Christ as the lion whose wrath is equally terrifying: “who can stand?” The fear of the king’s wrath is the temporal image of the fear of Christ’s judgment — yet the same one whose wrath is like the lion’s roar says to his people: “do not fear those who kill the body” (Matthew 10:28), providing the only standing-place before the divine wrath.
3: Honor in avoiding strife; every fool breaks out in quarreling. Christ as the peacemaker sets the standard: “blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God” (Matthew 5:9). He himself is “our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility” (Ephesians 2:14). The honor belonging to the one who avoids strife is the honor of the Son who came not to destroy but to reconcile (2 Corinthians 5:18-19).
4: The sluggard who doesn’t plow in autumn and seeks at harvest but finds nothing. Against all spiritual failure to prepare in the appointed season, Christ provides the only sustainable preparation: “seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you” (Matthew 6:33). The one who plows in the Spirit reaps eternal life (Galatians 6:8); the one who neglects the plowing of repentance and faith in the present season finds no harvest when the judgment comes.
5: Purpose in a man’s heart like deep water; the man of understanding draws it out. Christ as the man of understanding who drew out what was hidden in human hearts is illustrated throughout the Gospels: his questions to the disciples — “who do you say that I am?” (Matthew 16:15) — and to individuals draw out the deepest intentions and recognitions. John 2:25: “he himself knew what was in man.” He is the supreme counselor who draws from the deep waters of the human person what the person cannot reach alone.
6: “Many a man proclaims his own steadfast love, but a faithful man — who can find?” The rhetorical question about the rarity of reliable ḥesed receives its Christological answer: Christ is the faithful man who can be found. John 1:17: “grace and truth came through Jesus Christ.” 2 Timothy 2:13: “if we are faithless, he remains faithful — for he cannot deny himself.” The universally rare quality of human faithfulness is the exclusively certain quality of Christ — he is the only human being of whom the question “who can find?” receives a definitive positive answer.
7: The righteous man walking in integrity, his children blessed after him. Christ walks in perfect integrity — Hebrews 7:26: “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” — and his children are blessed through his integrity, not their own: “for as by the one man’s disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man’s obedience the many will be made righteous” (Romans 5:19). The children who walk after the father of integrity are the redeemed community blessed because of Christ’s perfect walk.
8: The king sitting on the judgment throne, winnowing all evil with his eyes. Matthew 25:31-33: “when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations, and he will separate people one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.” The king on the judgment throne winnowing evil with his eyes is the OT image whose eschatological fulfillment is Christ’s own royal judgment scene.
9: “Who can say, I have made my heart clean; I am pure from my sin?” — the rhetorical question implying no one. 1 John 1:8: “if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.” The one exception is Christ: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth” (1 Peter 2:22). He is the only human being who could truthfully say his heart was clean — and his clean heart is the righteousness imputed to those who cannot clean their own (2 Corinthians 5:21: “for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin”).
10: Diverse weights and measures as abomination to YHWH. Commercial dishonesty is not a peripheral sin but a structural covenant violation. Christ’s cleansing of the temple (Matthew 21:12-13; John 2:14-16) targeted precisely the commercial exploitation of the sacred — the money-changers who manipulated exchange rates within the house of prayer. His action demonstrates that YHWH’s abomination of commercial dishonesty extends into the center of the covenant community’s worship, not just the marketplace.
11: Even a child is known by his deeds — character is visible in action from the earliest years. Luke 2:41-52 records the one episode from Christ’s childhood preserved in the Gospels: the twelve-year-old Jesus in the temple, about his Father’s business. Even as a child his deeds already revealed his identity — the teachers who heard him were astonished (v47). The wisdom of Proverbs 20:11 applies to Jesus with its full force: his childhood deeds already disclosed the character that would be fully revealed in his public ministry.
12: The hearing ear and seeing eye — YHWH made them both. Christ as creator of all human faculties (John 1:3: “all things were made through him”) made the hearing ear and seeing eye. He also healed both in his ministry: “the blind receive their sight and the deaf hear” (Matthew 11:5, citing Isaiah 35:5-6). The Creator of hearing and sight demonstrates his identity by restoring what sin and death had damaged — the healed ear and opened eye are the sign that the Creator-God has come in flesh.
13: Love not sleep lest you come to poverty; open your eyes and have plenty of bread. Christ’s own ministry was characterized by active watchfulness: he often rose early to pray (Mark 1:35: “rising very early in the morning, while it was still dark, he departed and went to a desolate place and there he prayed”). John 6:35: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger.” The open eyes and plenty of bread promised by Proverbs meet their ultimate fulfillment in the one who is himself the bread of life for all who do not slumber in unbelief.
14: “Bad, bad,” says the buyer, then boasts — the commercial negotiator’s gap between professed and private evaluation. James 2:1-4 targets the same hypocrisy in the covenant community: showing partiality between rich and poor while professing equal regard. Christ exposed such hypocrisy consistently: “they say, and do not do” (Matthew 23:3). He himself maintained complete congruence between his words and his evaluations — no public deprecation and private approval, no flattery and hidden contempt.
15: Gold and costly stones abound, but lips of knowledge are the precious jewel. Christ’s lips of knowledge are the supreme precious jewel: “no one ever spoke like this man!” (John 7:46). Paul identifies the content of his speech as surpassing all other wealth: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord” (Philippians 3:8). The wisdom tradition’s valuation of speech over precious metals anticipates Paul’s declaration that the treasure of knowing Christ exceeds all calculated gain.
16: Take the garment of one who puts up security for a stranger. The pledge/surety dynamic (see Proverbs 6:1-5 and Hebrews 7:22) has its christological center in Christ as the ultimate guarantor. He who was the ultimate surety did not escape the taking of his garment — the soldiers cast lots for his clothing (John 19:23-24, fulfilling Psalms 22:18). The cosmic surety was stripped of his outer covering in his death, completing the pledge he had made for those he represented.
17: Bread of deceit is sweet but becomes gravel in the mouth. Against the false sweetness of deceptive gain, Christ offers true bread: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35). The gravel that replaces the deceptive bread’s sweetness is the inverse of the manna in the wilderness — gravel where bread should be. Christ is the true manna (John 6:31-35), the bread that does not become gravel but sustains to eternal life.
18: Plans established by counsel; by wise guidance wage war. Christ is the counselor of perfect wisdom (Isaiah 9:6: “Wonderful Counselor”) whose guidance guarantees victory. His own counsel for spiritual warfare is complete: “take up the whole armor of God” (Ephesians 6:11-17); “in the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world” (John 16:33). The wise guidance that Proverbs says establishes war-plans is the counsel that Christ as the Wonderful Counselor provides — and the victory he has already secured grounds the confidence of those who follow his guidance.
19: The gossip who reveals secrets — do not associate with a simple babbler. Christ’s counsel about trustworthy speech and the community of trust he forms inverts the gossip’s pattern. John 15:15: “no longer do I call you servants, for the servant does not know what his master is doing; but I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.” Christ shares the Father’s counsel with his disciples — the reverse of the gossip who reveals secrets harmfully; he reveals secrets (mysteries of the kingdom, Matthew 13:11) to those entrusted with them.
20: The lamp extinguished for cursing parents — the fifth commandment carried to its deadly endpoint. Christ perfectly honored his Father — “I always do the things that are pleasing to him” (John 8:29) — and is the eternal lamp: “the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb” (Revelation 21:23). The lamp that is extinguished for filial dishonor is the inverse of the Lamb who is the eternal light — his lamp cannot be extinguished because his filial obedience is perfect and complete.
21: An inheritance gained hastily in the beginning will not be blessed in the end. The prodigal son’s hastily demanded inheritance squandered in the far country (Luke 15:11-13) is the narrative illustration — and the father’s restoration of the returning son is the christological reversal. The unhastily gained inheritance is the one secured by Christ through patient suffering: “heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, provided we suffer with him in order that we may also be glorified with him” (Romans 8:17).
22: “Do not say, I will repay evil; wait for YHWH, and he will deliver you.” Romans 12:19: “beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, ‘Vengeance is mine, I will repay,’ says the Lord.” Christ himself performed this waiting: “when he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly” (1 Peter 2:23). His entrusting of himself to the Father-judge is the supreme example of the waiting that Proverbs commands.
23: Diverse weights again as abomination — the repetition emphasizes commercial covenant integrity. Christ’s final judgment will include an accounting of all commercial dealings: “for we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body, whether good or evil” (2 Corinthians 5:10). The YHWH who abhors false weights is the Christ who will weigh all human dealings with the perfect scale of divine justice.
24: “A man’s steps are from YHWH; how then can man understand his way?” Hebrews 12:2: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross.” Christ’s steps were entirely from the Father — he did nothing of his own initiative (John 5:30: “I can do nothing on my own... I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me”). His steps being perfectly from YHWH provides the model for discipleship: trusting the divine direction of one’s own path rather than grasping for autonomous understanding.
25: The trap of rash vows — sacred before reflection. Christ’s own counsel on vows addresses the same danger: “let what you say be simply ‘Yes’ or ‘No’” (Matthew 5:37). He himself made no rash vows but fulfilled perfectly the eternal covenant pledge he had made — not rashly but with complete foreknowledge of the cost (John 17:24: “Father, I desire that they also, whom you have given me, may be with me where I am”). His deliberate, foresighted commitment is the opposite of the snaring rash vow.
26: The wise king winnowing the wicked, driving the threshing wheel over them. Matthew 3:12 applies the winnowing image to Christ: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire.” The wise king of Proverbs 20:26 is the type; Christ is the eschatological fulfillment — the royal winnowing that sorts the wicked from the righteous at the last day.
27: “The spirit of man is the lamp of YHWH, searching all his innermost parts.” John 1:4: “in him was life, and the life was the light of men.” Christ is the source of the inner lamp — the breath/spirit that God breathed into man (Genesis 2:7) is renewed and relit in those who receive the Spirit that Christ sends (John 20:22: “he breathed on them and said to them, ‘Receive the Holy Spirit’”). The human spirit as YHWH’s lamp for inner searching becomes, in the Spirit-filled believer, the indwelling of the divine light itself.
28: “Steadfast love and faithfulness (ḥesed weʾěmet) preserve the king; by steadfast love his throne is upheld.” John 1:14: “we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth” — charin kai alētheian, the Greek rendering of ḥesed weʾěmet. Christ is the king whose throne is upheld by his own ḥesed and ʾěmet: Revelation 1:5 — “the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, and the ruler of kings on earth.” His rule is not sustained by force but by the covenant virtues that Proverbs identifies as royal foundations.
29: The glory of young men is their strength; the splendor of old men is their gray hair. Christ, the “Ancient of Days” prototype and the “one like a son of man” (Daniel 7:9, 13), encompasses both: the eternal wisdom of the Ancient of Days and the vigor of the incarnate Son. Revelation 1:14 describes the risen Christ with “hair white as white wool, as white as snow” — the white/gray of age — combined with eyes like flames of fire and a voice like the roar of many waters. He is the one in whom all human glories, of youth and age, find their completion and source.
30: Blows that wound cleanse away evil; stripes make clean the innermost parts. Isaiah 53:5: “he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed.” The wounds that cleanse in Proverbs 20:30 find their ultimate instance in Christ’s stripes: the blows that fell on him cleanse those he represents. The painful discipline that purifies in the wisdom tradition becomes at the cross the substitutionary wounding through which all inner cleansing flows.
